excerpt: You've installed a VPN. Now you're wondering if it's slowing you down. Here's how to test correctly and understand what the numbers mean.
author: VPN Adviser
content:
How to Test Your VPN Speed

You've installed a VPN. Now you're wondering if it's slowing you down. The question sounds simple: test before the VPN, test after, compare. But there are pitfalls. Most people run speed tests wrong, misinterpret the results, and then blame the VPN.

Why VPN Speed Matters

Gaming needs a ping under 50 milliseconds. Streaming 4K needs 25 Mbps. Torrenting wants the highest possible download speed. Your VPN will slow you down. The question is how much, and whether that slowdown matters for what you do.

How to Test Correctly

Run a speed test without the VPN connected. Use Speedtest.net or Fast.com. Run it three times and average. Now connect the VPN. Wait 10 seconds for the tunnel to stabilize. Run the same test three times. Average those results. Your VPN speed loss is the difference between baseline and VPN-connected results.

What the Numbers Mean

Download speed is how fast data comes to your computer. A 15% VPN-induced loss is typical. Upload speed shows how fast you send data. A 25-40% loss through a VPN is normal. Ping is how long a packet takes. For gaming, every millisecond counts. A VPN adding 20ms is acceptable. Jitter is variation in latency. This matters for voice calls and gaming.

Typical Speed Losses by Protocol

OpenVPN TCP: 30-60% loss. OpenVPN UDP: 15-30%. WireGuard: 5-15%. IKEv2: 10-20%.

Server Location Effect

Same country: 5-10% loss. Same continent: 15-25%. Different continent: 40-70%+. Distance adds latency. This is physics, not the VPN's fault.

When to Switch Servers

If getting less than 50% baseline speed, try a different server in the same country. The first server may be congested. Pick a green-bar server instead of red.

Bottom Line

Run a proper baseline test. Connect the VPN. Test again. Compare. If under 20% loss and you're not gaming competitively, you won't notice it.
title: How to Test Your VPN Speed (and What the Numbers Actually Mean)
